meta.title: Only Once Share — Secure One-Time Secret & Image Sharing
meta.description: Send passwords, images, API keys, and sensitive information securely with one-time self-destructing links. End-to-end AES-256 encryption, zero-knowledge architecture. Free and open source — no account required.

hero.title: Share secrets securely
hero.subtitle: End-to-end encrypted in your browser. The server never sees your data. Links self-destruct after one view.

security.metaTitle: How It Works — Security & Encryption | Only Once Share
security.metaDesc: Learn how Only Once Share protects your secrets with AES-256-GCM encryption, zero-knowledge architecture, and client-side key management.
security.title: How Only Once Share Keeps Your Secrets Safe
security.lead: Only Once Share uses end-to-end AES-256-GCM encryption with a zero-knowledge architecture. Your text and images are encrypted in your browser before they ever leave your device — our server never sees your plaintext data.
security.cta: Try Only Once Share — Free & Secure
security.e2eContent: When you create a secret, your browser generates a random cryptographic key using the Web Crypto API. Your text and any attached images are packed into a single encrypted payload using AES-256-GCM — the same encryption standard used by governments and financial institutions worldwide. The encryption happens entirely in your browser. The encrypted data (ciphertext) is the only thing sent to our server.
security.hkdfContent: Each secret gets its own unique encryption key derived using HKDF-SHA-256 (HMAC-based Key Derivation Function) with the secret's ID as context. This means even if someone obtained the master key material, each secret remains cryptographically independent. Compromising one secret cannot compromise another.
security.aadContent: The secret ID is bound as Additional Authenticated Data (AAD) during encryption. This cryptographically ties each encrypted payload to its specific secret ID. If anyone attempts to swap ciphertext between secrets or tamper with the secret ID, decryption will fail. This prevents a class of attacks where encrypted data could be substituted.
security.zkContent: Our server stores only encrypted blobs. We cannot read, decrypt, or access your secrets in any way. The decryption key is placed after the # symbol in the URL (the "fragment"). Browser URL fragments are never sent to servers — not in HTTP requests, not in server logs, not anywhere. This is a fundamental property of how web browsers work, defined in RFC 3986.
security.serverContent: Unlike some competing tools that perform encryption on the server (meaning the server briefly sees your plaintext data), Only Once Share encrypts entirely in the browser. The server never receives: your plaintext text or images, the encryption key, or any information that could be used to decrypt your data. It cannot even distinguish whether a secret contains text, an image, or both. Even if our server were compromised, your secrets would remain encrypted and unreadable.
security.oneTimeContent: When a recipient opens a secret link, the encrypted data is retrieved and atomically deleted from storage in the same Redis GETDEL operation. There is no window where a second read is possible. Additionally, all secrets automatically expire after the chosen TTL (1–72 hours) via Redis key expiration, even if never viewed. No data persists beyond the expiration window.
security.ossContent: Only Once Share is fully open source under the MIT license. Every line of code — from the encryption logic to the server handling — is publicly auditable on GitHub. You don't have to trust our claims; you can verify them yourself. You can also self-host the entire platform using Docker for complete control over your data.
security.step1: Key Generation — Your browser generates a random AES-256 key using the Web Crypto API
security.step2: Key Derivation — HKDF-SHA-256 derives a unique per-secret key using the secret ID as context
security.step3: Encryption — AES-256-GCM encrypts your text and images into a single payload with a random 96-bit IV and the secret ID as AAD
security.step4: Transmission — Only the encrypted ciphertext and secret ID are sent to the server
security.step5: Link Creation — The encryption key is placed in the URL fragment (#), which is never sent to the server
security.step6: Retrieval — When opened, the ciphertext is fetched and atomically deleted from Redis (GETDEL)
security.step7: Decryption — The recipient's browser decrypts the text and images using the key from the URL fragment
security.whyP1: Many secret sharing tools claim to be "encrypted" but perform encryption on the server. This means your plaintext secret travels over the network and is briefly visible to the server before encryption. Server-side encryption protects data at rest, but it requires you to trust the server operator.
security.whyP2: With client-side encryption (like Only Once Share), your text and images are encrypted before they leave your device. The server only ever handles encrypted data it cannot read. This is the gold standard for privacy and is the same principle behind end-to-end encrypted messaging apps like Signal.

about.metaTitle: About — Only Once Share | DHD Tech
about.metaDesc: Only Once Share is a free, open-source secret sharing tool built by DHD Tech. Learn about our mission to make secure credential sharing accessible to everyone.
about.title: About Only Once Share
about.lead: We built Only Once Share because sharing passwords and sensitive information shouldn't require trusting a third party with your data — or paying for the privilege.
about.missionP1: Every day, millions of people share passwords, API keys, and credentials through email, Slack, and text messages — channels that store data indefinitely and are vulnerable to breaches. We believe secure secret sharing should be free, simple, and truly private.
about.missionP2: Only Once Share was built to solve this problem with uncompromising security. Using AES-256-GCM encryption performed entirely in the browser, we created a tool where even we — the operators — cannot access your data. It's zero-knowledge by design, not by policy.
about.whyOssP1: Security tools that ask you to trust them without showing their work have a fundamental credibility problem. That's why Only Once Share is fully open source under the MIT license. You can read every line of code, audit the encryption implementation, and verify our zero-knowledge claims yourself.
about.whyOssP2: Open source also means you can self-host Only Once Share on your own infrastructure using Docker. Need to comply with data residency requirements? Run it in your own data center. Want to customize it for your team? Fork it and make it your own.
about.builtByP1: Only Once Share is built and maintained by DHD Tech, a software engineering team focused on building developer tools and security-first applications. We're passionate about privacy, cryptography, and making powerful tools accessible to everyone.
about.feature1Desc: Unlike tools that encrypt on the server, we encrypt in your browser. The encryption key lives only in the URL fragment — it's never transmitted to any server.
about.feature2Desc: No artificial limits. No "8 secrets per month." Share text up to 50,000 characters and images up to 10 MB, completely free, with no account required.
about.feature3Desc: Every line of code is public and auditable. Self-host on your own infrastructure with Docker. Contribute on GitHub.
about.feature4Desc: Available in English, Chinese, Spanish, Hindi, Arabic, and Portuguese. Secure sharing shouldn't have a language barrier.
about.involvedP1: Only Once Share is an open-source project and we welcome contributions. Whether it's reporting bugs, suggesting features, improving translations, or contributing code — every contribution makes secret sharing more secure and accessible for everyone.

faq.metaTitle: FAQ — Frequently Asked Questions | Only Once Share
faq.metaDesc: Frequently asked questions about Only Once Share: how encryption works, security architecture, self-hosting, pricing, and more.
faq.title: Frequently Asked Questions
faq.lead: Everything you need to know about Only Once Share's security, encryption, and features.
faq.q1: How does Only Once Share work?
faq.a1: When you create a secret — whether it's text, an image, or both — your browser generates a random AES-256-GCM encryption key using the Web Crypto API. Everything is encrypted entirely in your browser — the plaintext never leaves your device. The encrypted data is sent to our server and stored temporarily in Redis. The encryption key is placed in the URL fragment (after the # symbol), which browsers never send to servers. When the recipient opens the link, the encrypted data is retrieved and permanently deleted in a single atomic operation. Their browser then decrypts the secret using the key from the URL.
faq.a2: Yes. Only Once Share uses a zero-knowledge architecture, meaning our server only stores encrypted data it cannot read. The encryption key exists only in the URL fragment, which is never transmitted to any server — this is a fundamental property of how web browsers handle URL fragments (defined in RFC 3986). Even if our server were compromised, your secrets would remain encrypted and unreadable.
faq.q3: What encryption does Only Once Share use?
faq.a3: We use AES-256-GCM (Advanced Encryption Standard with 256-bit keys in Galois/Counter Mode), which is the same encryption standard used by governments, financial institutions, and military applications worldwide. Keys are derived using HKDF-SHA-256, and each secret gets a unique derived key. The secret ID is bound as Additional Authenticated Data (AAD) to prevent ciphertext substitution attacks.
faq.q4: Can the server see my secret?
faq.a4: No. Unlike some secret sharing tools that perform encryption on the server (where the server briefly handles your plaintext), Only Once Share encrypts everything in your browser before any data is transmitted. Our server only receives and stores encrypted ciphertext. The decryption key is never sent to the server — it stays in the URL fragment, which browsers do not include in HTTP requests.
faq.q5: What happens after someone views my secret?
faq.a5: The secret is permanently destroyed. When a recipient opens the link, the encrypted data is retrieved and atomically deleted from our Redis database using a GETDEL operation. There is no window where a second read is possible. The link will show "Secret Not Available" for any subsequent access attempt.
faq.a6: You can choose an expiration time between 1 and 72 hours (1h, 4h, 12h, 24h, 48h, or 72h). Secrets automatically expire after the chosen time even if they are never viewed. Once expired or viewed, the data is permanently deleted from our servers. No data persists beyond the expiration window.
faq.a7: Yes, completely free with no limits. Unlike some competitors that restrict free users to a handful of secrets per month or impose size caps, Only Once Share has no artificial limitations. You can share text secrets up to 50,000 characters and attach images up to 10 MB (JPEG, PNG, GIF, WebP), with no account required. The tool is also open source under the MIT license.
faq.q8: Do I need to create an account?
faq.a8: No. Only Once Share requires no registration, no email, no account. Just enter your text, optionally attach an image, choose an expiration time, and share the generated link. This zero-friction approach means there's no personal data to protect and no credentials that could be compromised.
faq.q9: Can I self-host Only Once Share?
faq.a9: Yes. Only Once Share is fully open source and designed for easy self-hosting with Docker. Clone the repository from GitHub, configure your environment variables, and run docker compose up. The entire stack (React frontend, Flask API, Redis storage) runs in containers. Self-hosting gives you complete control over your data and is ideal for organizations with data residency requirements.
faq.q10: Is Only Once Share open source?
faq.a10: Yes. The complete source code is available on GitHub under the MIT license. This means you can inspect every line of code, including the encryption implementation, to verify our security claims. You can also fork the project, modify it, and deploy your own version.
faq.q11: How is Only Once Share different from OneTimeSecret?
faq.a11: The key difference is encryption architecture. OneTimeSecret and some other tools encrypt secrets on the server, meaning the server handles your plaintext data before encrypting it. Only Once Share encrypts everything in your browser using the Web Crypto API — our server never sees your plaintext. Additionally, Only Once Share is completely free with no limits, supports 6 languages, and uses HKDF key derivation with AAD binding for stronger cryptographic guarantees.
faq.q12: What languages does Only Once Share support?
faq.a12: Only Once Share is available in 6 languages: English, Chinese, Spanish, Hindi, Arabic, and Portuguese. The language is automatically detected from your browser settings and can be changed using the language selector.

security.title: How It Works
security.e2eDesc: Your text and images are encrypted in your browser using AES-256-GCM with a random 96-bit IV before they ever leave your device. The encryption key is never sent to our server.
security.hkdfDesc: A unique encryption key is derived for each secret using HKDF-SHA-256 with the secret ID as context. Even with the master key, each secret has its own cryptographically independent key.
security.aadDesc: The secret ID is bound as Additional Authenticated Data (AAD) during encryption. If anyone tampers with the secret ID or swaps ciphertext between secrets, decryption will fail.
security.zkDesc: The server only stores encrypted data — it cannot distinguish text from images. We cannot read, decrypt, or access your secrets in any way. The decryption key exists only in the shared link's URL fragment.
security.keyDesc: The decryption key is placed after the # in the URL. Browser URL fragments are never sent to servers — not in requests, not in logs, not anywhere.
security.oneTimeDesc: When a secret is retrieved, it is atomically deleted from storage in the same operation. There is no window where a second read is possible.
security.expiryDesc: Secrets automatically expire after the chosen TTL (1–72 hours), even if never viewed. No data persists beyond the expiration window.
